problems.problem: Need cofounder: ML/software engineer for automation-first AI safety lab
problems.description: In the 'Open Thread 415' post, ACX grantee Jacob Arbeid is explicitly looking for a cofounder with strong software or ML engineering skills to build an AI safety lab. He describes organizational and engineering bottlenecks limiting safety tooling development and wants to build a lean organization focused on automating safety research with AI agents. This is a direct ask for a specific type of person to fill a clear role.
evidence: I'm looking for a strong software or ML engineer to cofound the world's first 'automation-first' AI safety lab.
As a founding member of the UK's AI Safety Institute, I saw firsthand how organisational, engineering and research bottlenecks limit humanity's ability to build the safety tooling we need.
I've received a generous grant from ACX to build this full-time, starting with AI evaluations. If this is something you feel should exist (no AI safety background required), reach out
To keep pace with AI's rapid capability advances, we'll need to go all-in on augmenting safety research and engineering with AI.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is an explicit ask with a Google Form and LinkedIn link provided. Jacob Arbeid has funding and is actively seeking a specific person to fill a critical role. A stranger with relevant ML/software engineering skills emailing about this would very likely get a response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Cofounder searches are typically urgent and time-bounded. With grant funding already secured, Jacob likely needs to find someone within a few months to maintain momentum, though the search could extend if the right candidate isn't found quickly.

problems.problem: Conditional prediction markets need better methodology to avoid confounding
problems.description: In "Mantic Monday: The Monkey's Paw Curls," the author explains that conditional/decision markets have a "fatal flaw" of confounding by noncausal pathways, making them unreliable for determining causal effects of policies. The author proposes a novel workaround using secondary markets to predict primary market prices on Election Day Eve, but acknowledges it doesn't completely solve the problem and requires mathematical validation beyond their ability.
evidence: The fatal flaw is confounding by noncausal pathways... Therefore, this can't be a good way to determine whether Republicans or Democrats will do a better job with the economy
Here's a potential workaround I've never seen before: suppose you create a set of conditional prediction markets as above. Then you create a set of secondary markets
This doesn't completely solve the conditional problem. There could be residual correlations based on hidden variables
Is there a way to simplify this so that we don't have to run all four markets?
problems.email_open_reasoning: The author has proposed a novel solution but explicitly states uncertainty about whether it fully works and whether it can be simplified. They acknowledge the limits of their mathematical ability to validate it. A stranger with relevant expertise in causal inference or market design might get a careful read, though this is more exploratory intellectual work than an urgent blocker.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The author is actively developing this idea (evident from working through examples with AI) and calls conditional markets 'the strongest case for prediction markets potentially being revolutionary.' This suggests sustained interest, though it's more theoretical exploration than an immediate project dependency.
problems.source_post: mantic monday the monkeys paw curls

problems.problem: Need user-created, subjective, real-money prediction markets (the Siskind Cube)
problems.description: In "Mantic Monday: The Monkey's Paw Curls," the author articulates frustration that despite prediction markets achieving high volume and legality, they haven't revolutionized society as hoped. The author identifies a specific gap: no platform offers real money markets that are user-created, user-resolved, subjectively managed (with creator revenue share), and easy to use in the US. The author has been requesting this "so long that Nuno Sempere dubbed it the Siskind Cube" and calls it "a billion dollar bill waiting to be picked up."
evidence: I've been asking for this so long that Nuno Sempere dubbed it the Siskind Cube

When I ask Manifold why they won't add 1 [real money], they say that Polymarket and Kalshi already dominate the space... When I ask Polymarket why they won't do 2 [user-created/resolved], the answer is a combination of regulatory issues, fear that people would write bad resolution criteria
I still think this is a billion dollar bill waiting to be picked up
Maybe the problem is: Polymarket and Kalshi don't have the right questions... why worry about them when you can go to the moon on sports bets?
problems.email_open_reasoning: While the author clearly cares about this problem and has written about it extensively, this is more of a long-standing wish than an urgent blocker. The author has already articulated the problem publicly and reached out to platforms directly. A stranger's email would likely be seen as 'yes, I know, but...' rather than offering new insights or solutions.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The author has been advocating for this for years (since at least 2021) and still sees it as important despite partial success in the space. However, the author also acknowledges AI superforecasters may make this less critical within 1-2 years, which could reduce urgency.
problems.source_post: mantic monday the monkeys paw curls
